guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines (eTG complete): Sjögren syndrome and management of xerostomia / sicca symptoms
eTG complete: Approach to acute dysphagia — exclude oesophageal food-bolus impaction and airway compromise
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present clinician who performed an oropharyngeal examination, palpated the parotid/submandibular glands, massaged Stensen's duct and observed the patient swallow 30 mL of water to exclude a mechanical food-bolus impaction and acute suppurative sialadenitis. Breath-Ezy CANNOT examine the mouth, throat or glands, so it must reach the disposition using patient-reportable discriminators (able to swallow water, no drooling, can manage own saliva, afebrile, no facial swelling) and safety-net the obstruction/airway/parotitis red flags.

Anchoring / premature-closure trap: a reassuring known background of Sjögren's sicca symptoms invites the AI to label tonight's acute dysphagia a benign flare and dismiss it, potentially missing an evolving oesophageal food-bolus impaction — a red-herring in either direction.

Alarm-symptom red herring: the alarming presenting phrases ('something is stuck', 'throat is burning intensely') mimic a mechanical obstruction, but the discriminating features (comfortable water swallowing, no drooling, no airway compromise) point away from true impaction; the case tests whether the AI over-weights the alarming narrative or the reassuring discriminators.

Age (~56) and sex (female) inferred — the source note states only weight (68 kg) and vitals, not age or sex; chosen conservatively given the strong female predominance and typical mid-life onset of primary Sjögren's syndrome; flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: AMS (Autoimmune Conditions Mild Severity), source note AMS-038.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
